# agent-coherence

> agent-coherence is an open-source coherence protocol for shared artifacts in multi-agent LLM systems. It **prevents the silent data-loss of a stale-read→write lost update** — when one agent or session reads an artifact, a peer commits a newer version, and the first writes back over it, the update is lost with no error. agent-coherence enforces single-writer ordering on a single host — for sequential and concurrent writers alike — serves the current version on the next read, and surfaces the divergence so the loss is never silent. Coordinating writers across multiple hosts is on the roadmap. It's also cheaper — ~12-token invalidation signals replace full-artifact rebroadcast — but correctness is the point. Implements a MESI-style cache-coherence protocol adapted from CPU cache design. A primary application is **RAG and shared agent memory**: those records are shared mutable state, so the same stale-view write silently clobbers a newer record there too — agent-coherence is the consistency layer under your retrieval/memory store, not another vector DB (see agent-coherence.dev/rag/).
>
> Ships as **two products** with one underlying engine:
>
> 1. **A Python library** (`agent-coherence`) with drop-in adapters for LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and custom orchestrators. Vendor-neutral across model providers.
> 2. **A Claude Code plugin** (`agent-coherence-plugin`) that wires the coherence protocol into Claude Code's hook system. Detects when one session's view of `CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, or other prose-shaped project rules drifts from another session's — or from what survives compaction.

## Positioning

The plugin's positioning one-liner (v0.1.1 marketplace listing, v0.1.1+ README, agent-coherence.dev/plugin): **"Coherence for the prose subset of project rules that can't be expressed as policy."**

This locates the plugin against the rules-as-prose vs rules-as-config structural framing:

| Class | Substrate | Right fix |
|---|---|---|
| Tool-class restrictions (e.g. `rg` over `grep`; no inline `python3 -c`) | `settings.json` `permissions.deny` — durable config | Migrate out of prose into config; survives subagent boundaries and compaction |
| Descriptive / architectural (`CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, `DECISIONS.md`) | Prose | Can't be config-ified by definition. Versioning + coherence tracking. **This is where the plugin sits.** |

The two fixes don't compete. Moving config-able rules to `permissions.deny` is hygiene that reduces the surface the plugin has to track. The prose residue — design constraints, architectural decisions, active-work artifacts — is what's left for coherence tracking.

## The library — what it does

- **Detects stale reads.** When agent B reads an artifact after agent A has modified it, agent-coherence flags the stale read instead of silently serving outdated data. As of **v0.10.0** this extends to **foreign edits**: a managed file changed out-of-band by a human, script, or tool not on the coordinator is caught when an agent re-reads it (denied in strict mode; opt-in via `CoherentVolume(on_stale_read="raise")`) — surfaced as `StaleView`, recovered via `reacquire()`. Most frameworks have no such mechanism; LangGraph's BaseStore documentation explicitly states it "does not natively support optimistic locking, vector versioning, or invalidation protocols."
- **Serves the current version on the next read.** Rather than broadcasting the full artifact to every agent on every turn (the default in CrewAI, AutoGen, and most LangGraph patterns), agent-coherence sends ~12-token invalidation signals when state changes and only re-fetches on the next read.
- **Enforces single-writer ordering — no silent lost updates on a single host.** Actor A reads v1, actor B commits v2, A then writes: A's write is denied (its cache went INVALID on B's commit) and A must re-read — the lost update is prevented, not silently applied. This holds for **both sequential and concurrent writers on a single host** — the concurrent same-key race is resolved by an optimistic commit-CAS plus fencing, so the loser gets a typed conflict and retries, never a silent drop. As of **v0.10.0**, a `CoherentVolume.write()` over a managed file that was edited out-of-band (a foreign edit the coordinator never saw) is also denied by default (`on_stale_write="raise"`), surfaced as `StaleView` and recovered via `reacquire()`. **Coordinating writers across multiple hosts is on the roadmap.** Single-writer is enforced by invalidation, not by an append-only reducer that produces duplicates or unexpected list nesting.
- **Records what each agent saw.** Per-agent content-audit log with SHA-256 hashes per read, plus a sequence-numbered state-transition log. The data substrate for run replay and stale-read forensics.
- **Exposes the coordinator over MCP (v0.10.0).** `stale-write-guard-fs` is a stdio MCP server (`pip install "agent-coherence[mcp]"`) that gives any MCP client the same protocol over five tools — `swg_read`, `swg_write`, `swg_reacquire`, `swg_write_cas` (single-shot version-checked CAS), and `swg_status`.

## The plugin — what it does

Aligns with Anthropic's published guidance for project-rule enforcement in Claude Code (Aug 2025, restated 2026): *"For deterministic behavior, your best bet is to use hooks."* The plugin is the hook implementation that closes the artifact-coherence gap.

- **Wires `PreToolUse` hooks across five tool surfaces** — `Read`, `Edit`/`Write` (shared matcher), `Bash` (file-path-aware via the `bash_path_detector`), and `Grep`. Five-handler coverage exists because a single-handler `Read`-only version is bypassable: when a Read warning fires, the model can retry 2–5 times then route around via `bash cat plan.md`, and without Bash + Grep coverage that bypass is silent. The wider surface is correctness for warn mode and a security floor for strict mode (v0.2).
- **On every covered tool call against a tracked artifact** the hook checks coordinator state. Warn mode (default) injects the `additionalContext` warning. Strict mode (v0.2, per-artifact opt-in via `.coherence/strict_mode.yaml`) returns `permissionDecision: "deny"` on a **stale-read attempt** (a tool-call read-deny, never global), with byte-identical text on every retry — **on the Python coordinator backend; in v0.2 the Node coordinator stays warn-only (v0.3 brings strict mode to Node).**
- **Per-workspace SQLite-backed coordinator.** Survives compaction. Survives crash. Re-hydrates on next session start.
- **`permissions.deny`-aware.** Doesn't double-warn on tools the config already blocks.

## The mechanism — one log line in the agent's own context

When a tracked artifact's version has changed since this session last observed it, the `PreToolUse:Read` hook injects a single sentence into the agent's `additionalContext`: the path, the version this session last saw, the version the workspace coordinator currently holds, and who last wrote it. The model reads that sentence in line with the read it's about to perform.

One of two outcomes follows, and both appear in the session transcript:

- **The agent acknowledges and re-reads.** The next `Read` tool call fetches current content. The agent's response references the divergence explicitly. The user sees the trace.
- **The agent doesn't acknowledge.** The warning was emitted; the model chose not to act on it. That choice is in the transcript too — discoverable on review, attributable to the model, not silent.

The mechanism applies to anything important loaded at the session-start boundary — `CLAUDE.md`, `plan.md`, `spec.md`, `runbook.md`, `DECISIONS.md`, skill instructions, slash-command prompts, project rules. Anything loaded at a boundary that compaction crosses inherits the same shape.

## Verification outside the agent loop

Sub-agent `completed` is a control-flow signal, not a semantic one. When a sub-agent returns claiming "I wrote file X," the runtime knows it returned — it doesn't know whether the write happened, or whether the content is what the parent expected.

The plugin's coordinator gives you a second source. After a sub-agent returns, the workspace coordinator either saw a `PostToolUse:Edit` event on the claimed file from that session — or it didn't. `agent-coherence-status` surfaces the coordinator's view independent of any prose in the agent's final message. One primitive of the many a "completion with verification" pattern would need; queryable by any orchestrator before it accepts the sub-agent's claim and moves on.

## What the plugin doesn't do

Claiming less than the plugin does is the cheaper credibility trade.

- **v0.1.1 is warn-only by default. v0.2 adds per-artifact strict-mode opt-in.** The plugin surfaces drift via `additionalContext` (warn mode, default; preserved byte-identical in v0.2 for any artifact not listed in `strict_mode.yaml`). Strict mode opts a tracked artifact into `permissionDecision: "deny"` on stale-read attempts — operator-controlled per-artifact via `.coherence/strict_mode.yaml`, never global. The Phase 0 falsifiability experiment (2026-05-19) confirmed the routing-around-Read mechanism that motivated the five-handler surface, and falsified the original "varied deny text bounds model retries" hypothesis — Opus reads varied text as prompt-injection patterns and retries more, not fewer. v0.2 deny text is therefore static and byte-identical across retries.
- **It doesn't fix model-internal compaction drift.** When the model decides which prior context to retain across a compaction boundary, the plugin has no influence — that's an inference-layer decision.
- **It doesn't fix inference-layer corruption.** If the model produces incorrect output despite having current artifact content in context, that's a model-quality problem, not a coherence problem.
- **It doesn't fix sandbox or permissions gaps.** Tool-class restrictions belong in `permissions.deny`; the plugin tracks the prose residue that survives config-ification.

The plugin addresses the cross-session and cross-subagent artifact-coherence layer — one piece of the structural pattern Anthropic has explicitly chosen to leave to userspace.

## What v0.2.0 added on top of v0.1.1

Tagged 2026-05-24; broad-beta open on the public marketplace; 14-day BB1–BB8 monitoring window in progress.

- **Per-artifact strict-mode opt-in** via `.coherence/strict_mode.yaml` — same shape as `tracked.yaml` and `ignored.yaml`. Intersection semantics with `tracked_paths`: an artifact must be tracked AND listed to enter strict mode. Startup footgun guard fires a warning if the strict-mode glob matches more than 50 tracked artifacts (default threshold).
- **`TERMINAL_DENIAL_CLASSES` structural security invariant.** Module-level frozenset enumerating denial classes that must never convert to `permissionDecision: "allow"`. All six allow-emission call sites route through `emit_allow()`, which refuses the conversion; an AST-based meta-test forces parameter-list extension on every new allow path so future contributors can't silently regress the boundary.
- **`agent-coherence-migrate-deny`** — a stricter sibling to v0.1.1's `agent-coherence-migrate-rules`. STDOUT-only (never writes `settings.json`), symlink-contained (canonical-path containment check refuses `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md` whose realpath escapes the workspace root), never invokes an LLM, under-emit bias (only canonical phrasings trigger). For security-sensitive operators.
- **Strict-mode telemetry.** Three counters in `/status?detail=metrics`: `strict_mode_denials_total`, `strict_mode_routed_around_via_bash_total` (30-second window detecting the Phase 0 H4 routing pattern), `audit_log_mode_drift_total`. Plus a minimal denial-only `.coherence/audit.log` in JSONL at mode 0o600 — no command bodies, no user content. Full callback surface deferred to a v0.2.x point release.
- **Cross-implementation protocol corpus.** `tests/protocol_corpus/` in the library repo, 12 warn-mode + 8 strict-mode fixtures parametrized over (fixture × backend). Catches Python ↔ Node coordinator wire-shape drift before it ships. Strict-mode fixtures are Python-only in v0.2 (the Node coordinator stays warn-mode; v0.3 brings strict mode to Node via the multi-target converter plan).
- **Broad-beta launch package.** README depth-parity overhaul (149 → 335 lines), `docs/BROAD_BETA.md` (BB1–BB8 rubric + 14-day monitoring window + rollback runbook), `CHANGELOG.md`, hardened public-feedback intake (three issue templates + four Discussion templates + `CODE_OF_CONDUCT` + `CONTRIBUTING` + `PRIVACY` + the `security@agent-coherence.dev` alias closes the v0.1.1 `SECURITY.md` TODO).

## What the library fits in coding-agent workflows

agent-coherence is the coherence layer for any setup where multiple agents share mutable artifacts:

- **Subagent fleets reviewing the same codebase** — style + security + architecture + synthesizer reviewers on the same files, with cache hits on the second-through-Nth read instead of re-reading from scratch.
- **Planner-executor pairs editing a shared task spec** — the planner re-plans mid-flight (writes v2 with a 4th caller); the executor's behavior depends on whether write-side coherence is on.
- **Refactor graphs fanning out across worktrees** — sub-agents touching the same files across sequential commits; the coordinator invalidates stale caches and enforces single-writer ordering so a later write can't silently land on top of a version it never saw — sequential or concurrent, on a single host. (Coordinating across multiple hosts is on the roadmap.)

## Measured impact (library)

### Simulation benchmarks

Reproducible on real LangGraph graphs in CI (uses `GenericFakeChatModel`; no live API calls):

| Workload | Reads:Writes | Cache hit rate | Token savings |
|---|---|---|---|
| Planning (read-heavy) | 12:1 | 75% | 69% |
| Code review (moderate) | 8:3 | 60% | 47% |
| High-churn (write-heavy) | 8:4 | 50% | 29% |

Reproduce: `make benchmark` after `pip install -e ".[langgraph,benchmark]"`.

### Real-LangGraph 4-agent code review

The repo ships `examples/shared_codebase/` as a working example: four reviewer subagents (style + security + architecture + synthesizer) read the same eight files in two passes each through `CCSStore`. Real LangGraph, deterministic fixture, no LLM API calls.

- 37.6% token reduction per run
- 16,820 tokens saved (44,702 → 27,882 per run)
- 35.3% cache hit rate over 51 read ops

Reproduce: `python -m examples.shared_codebase.main`.

### Recorded planner-executor refactor (three variants)

`examples/refactor_demo/` is a recorded demo showing two scripted sub-agents collaborating on a TypeScript refactor through `CCSStore`. The planner writes v1 of a task spec (rename 3 callers); the executor reads v1; the planner discovers a 4th caller and writes v2 mid-flight. Real `tsc --noEmit` against a real TypeScript fixture turns the correctness question into an on-screen build error.

Three variants assert the protocol mechanism + the application-level consequence:

- **`with`** — default `CCSStore`, lazy strategy. Executor reads v1 (cache SHARED), planner writes v2 (executor cache → INVALID), executor re-reads at commit → fetches v2. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS][MISS]`. Outcome: `committed spec v2 (5 files renamed)`, `tsc: OK`.
- **`no-invalidation`** — `disable_invalidation(store)` patches the event bus' `publish_invalidation` to no-op. Executor cache stays SHARED at v1; commit-time get is `[HIT]` on stale v1. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS][HIT]`. Outcome: `committed spec v1 (4 files)`, `tsc: FAIL TS2305` on `src/utils/session.ts`. **The protocol-level proof** — same code path, single-line bus suppression, different outcome.
- **`context-cache`** — executor never re-reads from the store; commits from the v1 spec it captured at read time. Mirrors LLM context-window behavior. Op log: `[MISS][MISS][MISS]` (only 3 ops; no commit-time read). Same `tsc: FAIL` outcome as `no-invalidation`. **The audience-facing demo** — same outcome as `no-invalidation`, with the mechanism (executor never re-reads) being what real LLM agents naturally exhibit in production.

Reproduce: `python -m examples.refactor_demo.main --variant=with|no-invalidation|context-cache`. See `outreach/demo/record.sh` in the website repo for the recording pipeline used on agent-coherence.dev/code.

## State-transitions visualization (the protocol in 4 beats)

The protocol's behavior on a single shared artifact, animated on agent-coherence.dev/code:

1. `t=0` — Planner writes v1 to the coordinator. Executor cache: empty.
2. `t=1` — Executor reads v1 from the coordinator. Executor cache: v1.
3. `t=2` — Planner writes v2. `CCSStore` publishes invalidation to peers before `write()` returns. Executor cache: INVALID.
4. `t=3` — Executor's next `get()` is MISS (cache invalidated); refetches v2. Executor cache: v2. `tsc` passes.

Single-writer ordering invariant: only one agent holds write permission on an artifact at a time. Monotonic versioning invariant: versions only increase. Both checked by `core/invariants.py` after every write/commit/invalidation.

## The trajectory framing (why this matters now)

Today's coding sub-agents run on **frozen specs**: the planner finishes, freezes a plan, then the executor runs. The two stages don't overlap, so coherence doesn't matter — the executor sees whatever the planner committed before exiting.

Tomorrow's coding sub-agents don't run that way. Deep Agents already does live re-planning. As soon as planners and executors run in parallel, frozen specs become stale specs, and stale specs become silent build failures. Coherence is what lets re-planning land in production without trampling the work already underway.

Pattern names showing up in production today: Claude Code Task subagent fleets, LangGraph code-review graphs with three or four reviewers on the same artifact, refactor pipelines fanning out planners and executors that edit a shared task spec. As of 2026, Anthropic's Agent View productized 10+ parallel Claude Code sessions in a single workspace — Research Preview, no documented state-isolation model.

## Install

**Library** (LangGraph / CrewAI / AutoGen / custom):

```
pip install "agent-coherence[langgraph]"
pip install "agent-coherence[crewai]"
pip install "agent-coherence[autogen]"
```

**MCP server** (`stale-write-guard-fs`, v0.10.0) — exposes the coordinator to any MCP client over stdio:

```
pip install "agent-coherence[mcp]"
```

Five tools: `swg_read`, `swg_write`, `swg_reacquire`, `swg_write_cas` (single-shot version-checked CAS), and `swg_status`.

**Plugin** (Claude Code). Two steps — the Python coordinator + hook client come from PyPI; the Claude Code plugin metadata and hook wiring come from the marketplace:

```
# Step 1 — Python coordinator + hook client (PyPI stable; the 0.8.0a1
#   pre-release alias is no longer needed since v0.8.0 stable shipped
#   2026-05-23). Warn mode works against v0.8.0+; strict mode (plugin v0.2)
#   requires the library's v0.9.0+ coordinator (current: v0.10.0).
pip install "agent-coherence>=0.8.0"

# Step 2 — Claude Code plugin
claude plugin marketplace add hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin
claude plugin install agent-coherence@agent-coherence

# Track an artifact and observe state
agent-coherence-track docs/plans/feature-x.md
agent-coherence-status
```

**Status:** v0.2.0 broad-beta tag pushed 2026-05-24 against the v0.1.1 marketplace-tested baseline (N=80 trials, 100% re-read-or-acknowledged, 0 ignored, on claude v2.1.131 with the haiku model). 14-day post-launch monitoring window in progress per the BB1–BB8 rubric documented at `agent-coherence-plugin/docs/BROAD_BETA.md`. v0.1.1 stays the recommended install for warn-mode-only deployments; v0.2.0 adds per-artifact strict-mode opt-in.

## LangGraph drop-in (library)

```python
from ccs.adapters import CCSStore        # instead of langgraph.store.memory.InMemoryStore

store = CCSStore(strategy="lazy")
graph = builder.compile(store=store)
```

No node-code changes. Coherence happens at the store layer.

## Synchronization strategies

Five strategies ship out of the box. Choose by workload shape:

- `lazy` (default) — re-fetch on next read. Cheapest. Best for read-heavy workloads with infrequent writes.
- `eager` — push updates to all readers on write. Best for systems where every agent must always have the latest version.
- `lease` — TTL-based grants. Bounded staleness. Best for medium-churn workloads.
- `access_count` — invalidate after N reads since last write. Best for predictable read patterns.
- `broadcast` — replicate writes to all peers. Comparable to the framework default; useful as a baseline.

## Vendor neutrality

The protocol operates on artifacts and version metadata, not on model responses or tokens. Behavior is identical with:

- **Model providers:** Anthropic (Claude), OpenAI (GPT), Google (Gemini), Mistral, AWS Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, open-source models via Ollama / vLLM / llama.cpp.
- **Orchestration frameworks:** LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and custom orchestrators via `CoherenceAdapterCore`. Plus Claude Code via the plugin.

## Why this exists — Anthropic on the gap

Anthropic's engineering team, after shipping their multi-agent Research system to production, named the gap in writing:

> *"This asynchronicity adds challenges in result coordination, **state consistency**, and error propagation across the subagents."*
> — Anthropic Engineering, *How we built our multi-agent research system* (June 2025)

agent-coherence is the protocol that addresses the state-consistency half of that observation. The Research system's appendix recommends the architecture too: *"Subagent output to a filesystem to minimize the 'game of telephone' [...] implement artifact systems where specialized agents can create outputs that persist independently."* The library's `CCSStore` is exactly that pattern, plus the coherence semantics that tell subagents when their cached view is stale.

## Memory and coherence are different layers

Every production agent runtime ships **durable execution, memory, guardrails, multi-tenancy, human oversight, observability, code execution, integrations, scheduling**. State coherence between concurrent agents writing to the same shared artifact is not in that list — because memory is keyed by thread, multi-tenancy is keyed by user, and neither of those answers the question *"what does agent B see when agent A just committed v3?"*

agent-coherence sits beside the runtime, not above or below. It composes with checkpoint-and-store memory the way locking composes with logging — different concerns, both required.

## Where this fits in the agentic stack

QuantumBlack/McKinsey's enterprise agentic platform architecture (April 2026) names a distinct layer of **agentic shared services** — the protocol-first, composable substrate between agent runtimes and enterprise data. agent-coherence is a state-consistency primitive in that layer, co-resident with agentic evaluation, observability, memory management, feedback loops, and security/protocol standards.

```
Agentic systems & runtimes        (MS AI Foundry, Google Vertex, AWS Bedrock,
                                   LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, custom)
   ↓
Interfaces & agentic orchestration (A2A, MCP, planner/supervisor patterns)
   ↓
Agentic shared services           ← agent-coherence is here
   ↓                              (state consistency · coherence protocol ·
                                   single-writer ordering)
In-house systems & external data  (knowledge graphs, RAG corpora, vector
                                   stores, read-side freshness pipelines)
```

Composable and protocol-first by design — slots into existing evaluations, observability, and memory layers without rewriting agent code or locking to a runtime.

## Two coherence problems — read-side vs write-side

The category split that frames the project:

- **Read-side freshness.** The world writes (commits, Slack, docs, tickets); agents read. You need an index pipeline that keeps the corpus current as sources change — incremental embeddings, knowledge graphs, retrieval. Examples: RAG over a codebase, search over a meeting archive.
- **Write-side coherence.** The agents themselves are the source of truth — they collaborate on shared plans, edit specs, mutate memory, hand off scratchpads. You need a coherence protocol that detects stale reads and enforces single-writer ordering when one agent commits. Examples: multi-agent planners, parallel sub-agents editing a spec, coding agents collaborating on a refactor, research crews mutating shared notes.

Both layers are needed in a real production system. agent-coherence focuses on the write side.

## RAG & shared agent memory (the read/memory arm)

RAG corpora and agent memory are shared mutable state — so the write-side lost update happens there too, and **a consistent store doesn't save you**: the staleness is in the *agent's cached view of a record*, not the store. Two agents read a record at v1; one writes v2; the other, still holding its v1, writes an edit computed from v1 and clobbers v2. The store did nothing wrong. agent-coherence keeps the readers honest — the moment one writes, the others' cached copies go INVALID before they can write a stale value back. Positioning + FAQ: https://agent-coherence.dev/rag/

- **Drop-in for memory/RAG stores.** `CCSStore` is a drop-in for `langgraph.store` (`get`/`put`/`search` unchanged) and composes with Mem0, Letta, LlamaIndex, a vector store, or a plain file. It does not embed, rank, or store vectors — it's the consistency layer underneath whatever you already use to retrieve and remember.
- **Runnable, deterministic demo (offline, no keys).** `python -m examples.coherent_volume.main` reproduces the documented lost update and then prevents it: the BROKEN run loses the update; the FIXED run denies the stale write fail-closed, reacquires, and both updates survive. A terminal recording of it is on the /rag page.
- **Honest scope.** Writes that go through the coordinator (a peer agent, another session, your store via `CCSStore`/`CoherentVolume`) are caught. As of **v0.10.0**, a *managed* file edited out-of-band — by a human, a script, or a tool not on the coordinator — is also caught at the agent's next access: on **re-read** (denied in strict mode; opt-in client surface `CoherentVolume(on_stale_read="raise")`) and on **write-over** (default-on, `on_stale_write="raise"`). Both surface as `StaleView`; recover via `reacquire()`. This is detection at the read/write boundary, not a background watcher — **proactively auto-watching an unmanaged source** that no agent reads or writes (an out-of-band re-index of a file outside the managed set) is still the source-watcher case, on the roadmap. The clean, shipped guarantee is the sequential stale-read→write lost update on a single host.
- **Cost dimension (TC-1).** A separate, pre-registered temporal-cost benchmark measures how many re-fetches coherence-gating avoids as a source drifts between turns. The metric is re-fetches-avoided — a proxy, reproducible from committed code in the repo, **not** a token/dollar invoice — kept as a repo artifact rather than a headline number (`benchmarks/cost_preregistration.md`, shipped in `v0.9.3`, current in `v0.10.0`).

## The coordination invariant

The plugin section above lays out the cross-cutting shape: the runtime emits coordination signals that don't reflect actual state. The same shape appears at three layers:

1. **Rules-as-prose layer.** `CLAUDE.md` isn't in context after compaction; subagents don't inherit it on spawn.
2. **ACK layer.** A notification fires before the underlying process terminates; a claim doesn't wait for readiness.
3. **Artifact-state layer.** One session's view of a shared file doesn't reflect another session's update.

agent-coherence is the artifact-layer instance: *signals about shared state must reflect actual state*. The plugin doesn't compete with runtime fixes for the other two layers — it sits at the layer those fixes don't reach: **cross-session and cross-subagent artifact coherence across worktrees and across mid-session artifact edits.**

## Failure modes the protocol catches

The named failure modes the audience already has vocabulary for — sourced from MLflow's multi-agent observability writeup, the LangGraph State of Agent Engineering survey, and engineering blogs from teams running multi-agent in production:

- **Stale read.** Agent B reads an artifact after agent A has modified it; B operates on outdated content. Most frameworks don't track per-agent ownership, so the read looks current. The plugin's `additionalContext` warning fires before the read; the library's MESI state machine flags the cache as INVALID.
- **Shared memory pollution.** One agent's hallucination becomes a "fact" subsequent agents reason from. Specifically: agent A writes incorrect content; agent B reads it as ground truth; agent C reads B's downstream work; the original error has now propagated three deep. Coherence semantics make the source of every read inspectable.
- **Cascading error.** A specialist sub-agent misinterprets constraints; downstream agents incorporate the error into their analyses; the mistake compounds through multiple reasoning steps before detection. With per-agent state tracking, the divergence point is identifiable post-hoc.
- **Context handoff drift.** Information loss between sub-agents that share artifacts through truncated messages or summary contexts. The artifact-coordination layer treats handoffs as version transitions, not as opaque message-passing.
- **Stale-read → lost update.** Actor A reads v1, a peer commits v2, A writes back over v2 — the update is lost, silently. The protocol enforces single-writer ordering so it doesn't: A's write is denied (its cache went INVALID) and A must re-read — for sequential and concurrent writers on a single host alike (the concurrent race resolved by optimistic commit-CAS + fencing; the loser retries, never a silent drop). Coordinating across multiple hosts is on the roadmap. Single-writer is enforced by invalidation, not by an append-only reducer that produces duplicates or unexpected list nesting.

## TAM beyond CLAUDE.md

The same fragility applies to skill instructions and slash-command prompts loaded at session start. Anything important loaded at the session boundary — which is to say, anything loaded at a boundary that compaction crosses — has the same shape. `CLAUDE.md` is the visible case; every plugin that loads instructions at session start (slash commands, skill prompts, project rules) inherits it.

## Production safety

- **License:** Apache-2.0, public on GitHub. Both the library and the plugin.
- **Tests (library):** ~1,290 unit + integration tests. Plus an opt-in launch-gate (N=40 live-claude scenarios × multi-model matrix in v0.2) and a cross-implementation protocol corpus that catches Python ↔ Node coordinator wire-shape drift across 31 scenarios with strict-mode + warn-mode coverage.
- **Formal verification:** Protocol safety properties (single-writer, monotonic versioning, no torn reads) model-checked with TLA+/TLC in CI.
- **Supply chain:** PyPI Trusted Publishers (OIDC), PEP 740 attestations, CycloneDX SBOM published with every release.
- **Crash recovery:** Optional sweep reclaims stale grants when agents OOM-kill or livelock (heartbeat-based, opt-in via `CrashRecoveryConfig`). The plugin's SQLite coordinator rehydrates after idle-shutdown.
- **Diagnose CLI:** `ccs-diagnose` is a zero-network static analyzer that detects stale-read risk in existing LangGraph graphs before adoption.

## Key links

- **Library source:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence
- **Library PyPI:** https://pypi.org/project/agent-coherence/
- **Plugin source:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin
- **Plugin CHANGELOG:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md
- **Plugin broad-beta playbook:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/blob/main/docs/BROAD_BETA.md
- **Plugin contributing guide:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md
- **Plugin privacy statement:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/blob/main/PRIVACY.md
- **Paper:** https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.15183
- **Home:** https://agent-coherence.dev
- **Library landing page:** https://agent-coherence.dev/code/
- **Plugin landing page:** https://agent-coherence.dev/plugin/
- **RAG & shared-memory landing page:** https://agent-coherence.dev/rag/

- **Plugin discussions:** https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/discussions
- **Contact:** contact@agent-coherence.dev
- **Security:** security@agent-coherence.dev (72h response SLA) or https://github.com/hipvlady/agent-coherence-plugin/security
- **15-min call:** https://cal.com/agent-coherence
- **LinkedIn:** https://www.linkedin.com/company/agent-coherence/

## Background reading

- `docs/why-coherence-matters.md` — public evidence of the consistency gap across LangGraph, CrewAI, AutoGen, and Claude Agent SDK, with linked sources.
- `docs/agent-coherence-approach.md` — how the MESI-derived approach maps to the documented gaps.
- arXiv 2603.15183 — formal protocol specification, simulation results, and proofs.
